text: Beginners should perform 10-12 total sets per muscle group per week. Exceeding this increases injury risk without additional hypertrophy benefits.
New lifters should focus on compound movements like squats, deadlifts, bench press, and rows for the first 6 months of training.
Intermediate lifters can handle 12-16 sets per muscle group per week with adequate recovery between sessions.
After 1-2 years of training, you can add isolation exercises to target specific muscle weaknesses identified in your assessment.
Advanced lifters can train with 16-22 sets per muscle group per week, using periodization to manage fatigue.
Always allow 48-72 hours between training the same muscle group to prevent overtraining and allow for proper recovery.
Never train to absolute failure on compound movements like squats and deadlifts. Leave 1-2 reps in reserve to maintain form.
Warm up with 2-3 light sets before working sets to prepare joints and muscles, reducing injury risk.
Sleep 7-9 hours per night for optimal muscle recovery and growth hormone release during deep sleep phases.
Consume 1.6-2.2g of protein per kg of bodyweight daily to support muscle protein synthesis and recovery.
Deload every 4-6 weeks by reducing volume or intensity by 40-50% to allow accumulated fatigue to dissipate.
Prioritize rear delts and upper back if shoulders appear rounded forward. Include face pulls and reverse flyes.
If chest is underdeveloped, focus on progressive overload on bench press variations and add cable flyes for stretch.
For arm development, prioritize compound movements first, then add direct bicep and tricep work with 6-10 weekly sets each.
Train muscles bilaterally to prevent asymmetry. Use unilateral exercises like lunges and single-arm rows to correct imbalances.
Users with lower back pain should avoid traditional deadlifts. Replace with hip hinges like Romanian deadlifts with lighter weight.
For lower back issues, strengthen core with planks and bird-dogs. Avoid sit-ups and twisting movements under load.
Users with knee pain should avoid deep squats and leg extensions. Use box squats and leg press with controlled range of motion.
Users with shoulder injuries should avoid behind-the-neck presses and upright rows. Use neutral grip pressing and lateral raises below 90 degrees.
For rotator cuff issues, include external rotation exercises with bands and avoid sleeping on the affected shoulder.
Use double progression: increase reps within a range (8-12), then add weight when you hit the top of the range.
Beginners can add weight weekly. Intermediate lifters may only progress every 2-4 weeks on compound lifts.
Beginners should use full-body workouts 3 times per week, or upper/lower split 4 times per week for optimal frequency.
Intermediate lifters benefit from push/pull/legs or upper/lower splits training each muscle 2x per week.
Advanced lifters can use bro-splits or high-frequency programs depending on recovery capacity and goals.
